Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.content: 106 cells changed. The animation shows the trail changed again. Let me check the current state - I can see from the image that the white-bordered has reappeared somewhere near the center, and the orange-bordered magenta moved.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Key objects after clicking orange magenta at (18,38):")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 25 and n['color'] not in ('B', 'g'):
        center_row = sum(b[0] for b in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        center_col = sum(b[1] for b in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} ~({center_row},{center_col}) children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

After clicking the orange-bordered magenta at (18,38):
- White-bordered appeared at (18,38) with orange center (id=24, color=W, pixels=12)
- Orange-bordered magenta moved from (18,38) to (11,34) (id=12, color=O, pixels=20)
- The trail restructured again
So clicking the orange-bordered magenta at (18,38) moved the white-bordered to (18,38) and the orange-bordered magenta moved to (11,34).
Now the state is:
- White-bordered at (18,38) with orange center
- Gray-bordered at (6,17) with orange center
- Gray-bordered at (9,49) with orange center
- Orange-bordered magenta at (11,34)
- Gray-bordered at (35,45) with purple center
- Purple-bordered magenta at (41,49)
- Gray-bordered at (48,54) with purple center
- Orange diamond ring at bottom
- Purple diamond ring at right
So now white-bordered is at (18,38), which is roughly in the center of the board. The orange-bordered magenta is at (11,34), which is between (18,38) and the upper gray-bordered objects.
The trail now seems to connect: (6,17) ← (11,34) ← (18,38) ← (9,49) and also (18,38) connects to the purple trail somehow.
Wait, but the purple trail is separate: (35,45) ← (41,49) ← (48,54).
Let me think about the goal. In Level 1, the goal was to get the white-bordered to the ring. In Level 2, there are two rings: orange and purple.
Maybe the goal is to get the white-bordered (orange center) to the orange ring?
Currently white-bordered is at (18,38). The orange ring is at the bottom. I need to move white toward the orange ring.
In Level 1, clicking magenta moved white one step along the trail. Let me try clicking the orange-bordered magenta at (11,34) to see if it moves white.

action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 11, 'col': 34}], intent='solve')
</parameter>
